data.examName: AP 微观经济 2018年国际卷

questionList.questionTitle: Economics exists because of scarcity
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: The study of economics exists because people are confronted with the basic problem of

optionList.optionContent: elasticity
negative externality
scarcity
overabundance
monopoly

questionOption:
A .

elasticity

B .

negative externality

C .

scarcity

D .

overabundance

E .

monopoly

questionList.questionTitle: Identify optimal quantity of pollution abatement
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: The table above shows the total benefit associated with reducing different levels of pollution on a small island. The cost of eliminating one ton of pollution is $150. Which of the following is the optimal quantity of pollution to eliminate?

optionList.optionContent: 5 tons
4 tons
3 tons
2 tons
1 tons

questionOption:
A .

5 tons

B .

4 tons

C .

3 tons

D .

2 tons

E .

1 tons

questionList.questionTitle: Identify government cost of subsidized price floor
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: The diagram above depicts the demand and supply curves for good Y. The perfectly competitive market is initially in equilibrium, but the government decides to institute a price floor program by setting the price of good Y at PF and agrees to purchase all unsold units of good Y from the producers. The cost of this program to the government is represented by which of the following areas?

optionList.optionContent: 0PFJQ2
0PFKQ3
GIKH
Q1IJQ2
Q1IKQ3

questionOption:
A .

0PFJQ2

B .

0PFKQ3

C .

GIKH

D .

Q1IJQ2

E .

Q1IKQ3

the demand for a substitute good
the supply of the good
relative price
marginal utility

consumers’ purchasing power

C .

questionList.questionTitle: Bruce's economic profit as artist is -$20,000
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Bruce is a talented writer and graphic artist who enjoys both types of work equally. Instead of earning $45,000 as a writer, Bruce now earns $25,000 in accounting profits as a graphic artist using the same computer equipment he would have used as a writer. What is Bruce’s economic profit from choosing to work as a graphic artist?

optionList.optionContent: -$45,000
-$20,000
$20,000
$45,000
$70,000

questionOption:
A .

-$45,000

B .

-$20,000

C .

$20,000

D .

$45,000

E .

$70,000

questionList.questionTitle: Game theory most useful with interdependent firms
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Game theory is most useful in describing outcomes in markets where

optionList.optionContent: firms are price takers
there is only one producer
there are many small producers
products are identical for all firms
there are interdependent firms

questionOption:
A .

firms are price takers

B .

there is only one producer

C .

there are many small producers

D .

products are identical for all firms

E .

there are interdependent firms

questionList.questionTitle: Firms in PC produce identical products
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Which of the following is a characteristic of firms in a perfectly competitive industry?

optionList.optionContent: Engaging in collusive behavior
Facing significant barriers to entry
Setting the market price
Producing identical products
Earning positive long-run economic profit

questionOption:
A .

Engaging in collusive behavior

B .

Facing significant barriers to entry

C .

Setting the market price

D .

Producing identical products

E .

Earning positive long-run economic profit

questionList.questionTitle: MC above AVC is firm's short-run supply curve
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A perfectly competitive firm’s short-run supply curve is the portion of the marginal cost curve that is

optionList.optionContent: above the average total cost curve
above the average variable cost curve
above the minimum average fixed cost curve
above the demand curve
between the average fixed cost curve and the average variable cost curve

questionOption:
A .

above the average total cost curve

B .

above the average variable cost curve

C .

above the minimum average fixed cost curve

D .

above the demand curve

E .

between the average fixed cost curve and the average variable cost curve

questionList.questionTitle: Segregate buyers by elasticity to price discrim
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Which of the following is a necessary condition for price discrimination?

optionList.optionContent: Resale of the product or service is possible.
Buyers have identical elasticities of demand.
The seller can separate consumers according to their elasticities of demand.
The seller is a perfectly competitive firm.
Buyers are aware of prices charged to other buyers.

questionOption:
A .

Resale of the product or service is possible.

B .

Buyers have identical elasticities of demand.

C .

The seller can separate consumers according to their elasticities of demand.

D .

The seller is a perfectly competitive firm.

E .

Buyers are aware of prices charged to other buyers.

questionList.questionTitle: Better education means higher wages for coders
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Which of the following would cause the wage for computer programmers to increase?

optionList.optionContent: A decrease in the amount of physical capital per computer programmer
An improvement in the education of computer programmers
An increase in the social prestige associated with being a computer programmer
A decrease in the value that computer programmers place on leisure
A minimum wage set below the equilibrium wage in the market for computer programmers

questionOption:
A .

A decrease in the amount of physical capital per computer programmer

B .

An improvement in the education of computer programmers

C .
An increase in the social prestige associated with being a computer programmer

D .

A decrease in the value that computer programmers place on leisure

E .

A minimum wage set below the equilibrium wage in the market for computer programmers

questionList.questionTitle: MSC equals MSB socially optimal Q of public good
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: The socially optimal quantity of a public good is provided when

optionList.optionContent: marginal private cost equals marginal private benefit
marginal social cost equals marginal social benefit
total private cost equals total social benefit
total social cost equals total social benefit
marginal social benefit minus marginal social cost is at a maximum

questionOption:
A .

marginal private cost equals marginal private benefit

B .

marginal social cost equals marginal social benefit

C .

total private cost equals total social benefit

D .

total social cost equals total social benefit

E .

marginal social benefit minus marginal social cost is at a maximum

questionList.questionTitle: Command economies, more govt resource ownership
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Command economies differ from capitalist economies mainly because of which of the following?

optionList.optionContent: Command economies have higher prices for oil.
Capitalist economies have higher prices for oil.
Command economies have higher degree of government ownership of resources.
Capitalist economies have higher degree of government ownership of resources.
Command economies rely on prices to allocate resources.

questionOption:
A .

Command economies have higher prices for oil.

B .

Capitalist economies have higher prices for oil.

C .

Command economies have higher degree of government ownership of resources.

D .

Capitalist economies have higher degree of government ownership of resources.

E .

Command economies rely on prices to allocate resources.

questionList.questionTitle: Price ceiling above equilibrium has no impact
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: In the rental housing market, what would be the effect of a rent control that was set at the market equilibrium rental price?

optionList.optionContent: It would increase the number of housing units available, creating a surplus.
It would decrease the number of housing units available, creating a shortage.
It would have no impact on the number of housing units available.
It would increase the consumer surplus and decrease the producer surplus.
It would decrease the consumer surplus and increase the producer surplus.

questionOption:
A .

It would increase the number of housing units available, creating a surplus.

B .

It would decrease the number of housing units available, creating a shortage.

C .

It would have no impact on the number of housing units available.

D .

It would increase the consumer surplus and decrease the producer surplus.

E .

It would decrease the consumer surplus and increase the producer surplus.

questionList.questionTitle: Impact of supply shift when D is perfectly elastic
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: If the demand for product X is perfectly elastic and the supply of product X decreases, which of the following will occur in the market for X ?

optionList.optionContent: Total revenue will increase.
Total revenue will decrease.
Total revenue will remain the same.
Quantity demanded will exceed quantity supplied.
Quantity supplied will exceed quantity demanded.

questionOption:
A .

Total revenue will increase.

B .

Total revenue will decrease.

C .

Total revenue will remain the same.

D .

Quantity demanded will exceed quantity supplied.

E .

Quantity supplied will exceed quantity demanded.

questionList.questionTitle: Decrease in price doesn't shift demand
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: All of the following cause a rightward shift in the demand schedule for a normal good EXCEPT

optionList.optionContent: a decrease in the price of the good
a decrease in the price of a complementary good
an increase in the price of a substitute good
an increase in consumers’ income
an increase in consumers’ preference for the good

questionOption:
A .

a decrease in the price of the good

B .

a decrease in the price of a complementary good

C .

an increase in the price of a substitute good

D .

an increase in consumers’ income

E .

an increase in consumers’ preference for the good

questionList.choiceQuestionContent: The graphs above show Mary’s demand for hamburgers and Mark’s demand for hamburgers. Suppose Mary and Mark are the only two consumers in the market. Which of the following is a point on the market demand curve for hamburgers?

questionList.questionTitle: Calculate price of capital at $5 from data
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A firm is currently employing a cost-minimizing combination of labor and capital for a given level of output. The firm is employing 20 workers, and the marginal product of the last worker is 40 units of output. The firm is employing 100 units of capital, and the marginal product of the last unit of capital is 10 units of output. If the wage rate is $20 per hour, what is the price of capital?

optionList.optionContent: $2.5 per hour
$5 per hour
$10 per hour
$100 per hour
$200 per hour

questionOption:
A .

$2.5 per hour

B .

$5 per hour

C .

$10 per hour

D .

$100 per hour

E .

$200 per hour

questionList.questionTitle: Diminishing MP causes upward MC
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: If a firm’s production function exhibits diminishing marginal product of the variable input in the short run, which of the following about the firm’s short-run marginal cost (MC) curve must be true?

optionList.optionContent: As output increases, the MC curve slopes upward.
As output increases, the MC curve slopes downward and becomes flatter.
As output increases, the MC curve slopes downward and becomes steeper.
The MC curve is horizontal.
The MC curve is vertical.

questionOption:
A .

As output increases, the MC curve slopes upward.

B .

As output increases, the MC curve slopes downward and becomes flatter.

C .

As output increases, the MC curve slopes downward and becomes steeper.

D .

The MC curve is horizontal.

E .

The MC curve is vertical.

questionList.questionTitle: Rising price should drive up Catering Inc output
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Catering, Inc., which provides catering services in a perfectly competitive market, was maximizing profits at the market price of $22 per meal. The market price has recently increased to $28 per meal. Which of the following short-run adjustments will increase profits for Catering, Inc.?

optionList.optionContent: Increasing output until the marginal cost equals the new price
Increasing output until the average total cost equals the new price
Decreasing output because of the increase in market price
Increasing the wage rate paid to its workers
Increasing advertising to increase demand for the meals

questionOption:
A .

Increasing output until the marginal cost equals the new price

B .

Increasing output until the average total cost equals the new price

C .

Decreasing output because of the increase in market price

D .

Increasing the wage rate paid to its workers

E .

Increasing advertising to increase demand for the meals

questionList.questionTitle: Mono comp inefficient because P exceeds MC
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Monopolistically competitive product markets are inefficient because

optionList.optionContent: price equals the marginal value to the buyer of the last item produced
price is greater than marginal cost
excessive competition prevents other firms from entering the market
homogeneous goods are usually overpriced
short-run economic profit-making opportunities exist

questionOption:
A .

price equals the marginal value to the buyer of the last item produced

B .

price is greater than marginal cost

C .

excessive competition prevents other firms from entering the market

D .

homogeneous goods are usually overpriced

E .

short-run economic profit-making opportunities exist

questionList.questionTitle: Nash Equilibrium for North Springs, South Springs
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
North Springs and South Springs are two firms that serve the local market for bottled water. Each firm must choose to either maintain or increase its current output. The first entry in each cell in the payoff matrix below shows the profits for North Springs, and the second entry in each cell shows the profits for South Springs.

If the two firms do not cooperate, which of the following represents the payoff North Springs and South Springs receive in the dominant-strategy equilibrium and the Nash equilibrium?

optionList.optionContent:
Dominant StrategyNash Equilibrium
None($27, $28)

Dominant StrategyNash Equilibrium
$27, $28)($15, $45)

Dominant StrategyNash Equilibrium
($30, $26)($15, $45)

Dominant StrategyNash Equilibrium
($27, $28)($27, $28)

Dominant StrategyNash Equilibrium
($30, $26)($27, $28)

questionOption:
A .
Dominant StrategyNash Equilibrium
None($27, $28)

B .
Dominant StrategyNash Equilibrium
$27, $28)($15, $45)

C .
Dominant StrategyNash Equilibrium
($30, $26)($15, $45)

D .
Dominant StrategyNash Equilibrium
($27, $28)($27, $28)

E .
Dominant StrategyNash Equilibrium
($30, $26)($27, $28)

questionList.questionTitle: Market demand curve is monopolist demand curve
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A monopolist’s demand curve is necessarily

optionList.optionContent: the same as the market demand curve
the same as its marginal revenue curve
upward sloping
perfectly elastic
perfectly inelastic

the same as the market demand curve

the same as its marginal revenue curve

C .

D .

questionList.questionTitle: Calculate MC and AVC from firm data
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A profit-maximizing, perfectly competitive firm is currently in long-run equilibrium. It is earning $15,000 of total revenue from a sale of 1,000 units. Its total fixed cost of production is $2,500. Which of the following can correctly be inferred from the information provided?

optionList.optionContent: Its marginal cost is $12.50, and its average total cost is $12.50.
Its marginal cost is $12.50, and its average variable cost is $12.50.
Its marginal cost is $15.00, and its average total cost is $12.50.
Its marginal cost is $15.00, and its average variable cost is $12.50.
Its marginal cost is $15.00, and its average fixed cost is $12.50.

questionOption:
A .

Its marginal cost is $12.50, and its average total cost is $12.50.

B .
Its marginal cost is $12.50, and its average variable cost is $12.50.

C .

Its marginal cost is $15.00, and its average total cost is $12.50.

D .

Its marginal cost is $15.00, and its average variable cost is $12.50.

E .

Its marginal cost is $15.00, and its average fixed cost is $12.50.

optionList.optionContent: A decrease in immigration restrictions
A decrease in the cost of child care
An increase in the number of remote-working jobs
An increase in preference for leisure
An increase in wages

questionOption:
A .

A decrease in immigration restrictions

B .

A decrease in the cost of child care

C .

An increase in the number of remote-working jobs

D .

An increase in preference for leisure

E .

An increase in wages

questionList.questionTitle: Find per-unit tax to address negative externality
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
The following questions refer to the following diagram, which shows the marginal social cost (MSC), the marginal private cost (MPC), and the marginal social benefit (MSB) associated with the production of a good in a market.

The government can attempt to correct the externality by setting a

optionList.optionContent: per-unit tax equal to (P4 − P1)
per-unit tax equal to (P4 − P2)
per-unit subsidy equal to (P4 − P2)
per-unit subsidy equal to (P3 − P2)
price ceiling at P2

questionOption:
A .

per-unit tax equal to (P4 − P1)

B .

per-unit tax equal to (P4 − P2)

C .

per-unit subsidy equal to (P4 − P2)

D .

per-unit subsidy equal to (P3 − P2)

E .

price ceiling at P2

questionList.questionTitle: Amy and Brad produce apple pie, ice cream
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Both Amy and Brad produce and consume apple pie and ice cream. In one hour, Brad makes five apple pies or ten gallons of ice cream while Amy makes fifteen apple pies or fifteen gallons of ice cream. Based on the above information, one can correctly conclude that

optionList.optionContent: Brad has an absolute advantage in making ice cream and will sell ice cream to Amy
Brad has a comparative advantage in making ice cream and will sell ice cream to Amy
Brad does not have a comparative advantage in making either good and will not trade with Amy
Brad has an absolute advantage in making apple pies but a comparative advantage in making ice cream
Brad’s opportunity cost of making ice cream is higher than Amy’s

questionOption:
A .

Brad has an absolute advantage in making ice cream and will sell ice cream to Amy

B .

Brad has a comparative advantage in making ice cream and will sell ice cream to Amy

C .

Brad does not have a comparative advantage in making either good and will not trade with Amy

D .

Brad has an absolute advantage in making apple pies but a comparative advantage in making ice cream

E .

Brad’s opportunity cost of making ice cream is higher than Amy’s

questionList.questionTitle: Tax on computers decreases consumer surplus
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: If the government imposes a per-unit tax on personal computers, consumer surplus in the personal computer market will

optionList.optionContent: decrease, because the price paid by consumers for personal computers will have increased
decrease, because there will be more personal computers on the market
increase, because personal computer manufacturers will pass the tax on to consumers
increase, because consumers will buy more imported personal computers
not change because the demand is unit elastic

questionOption:
A .

decrease, because the price paid by consumers for personal computers will have increased

B .

decrease, because there will be more personal computers on the market

C .

increase, because personal computer manufacturers will pass the tax on to consumers

D .

increase, because consumers will buy more imported personal computers

E .

not change because the demand is unit elastic

questionList.questionTitle: Elasticity and tax incidence in corn market
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Assume that the supply of corn is relatively price inelastic, while the demand for corn is relatively price elastic. If the government imposes a per-unit excise tax on the production of corn, the incidence of the tax will fall

optionList.optionContent: entirely on buyers
more on buyers than on sellers
entirely on sellers
more on sellers than on buyers
equally on both buyers and sellers

questionOption:
A .

entirely on buyers

B .

more on buyers than on sellers

C .

entirely on sellers

D .

more on sellers than on buyers

E .

equally on both buyers and sellers

Quantity Consumed Marginal Utility

20

questionList.questionTitle: Decreasing LRATC illustrates economies of scale
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Economies of scale can be illustrated by

optionList.optionContent: an increasing short-run marginal cost curve as a firm produces more output
a decreasing short-run average total cost curve as a firm produces more output
a downward-sloping long-run supply curve for an industry
a decreasing long-run average total cost curve as a firm produces more output
an increasing long-run average total cost curve as a firm produces more output

questionOption:
A .

an increasing short-run marginal cost curve as a firm produces more output

B .

a decreasing short-run average total cost curve as a firm produces more output

C .

a downward-sloping long-run supply curve for an industry

D .

a decreasing long-run average total cost curve as a firm produces more output

E .

an increasing long-run average total cost curve as a firm produces more output

questionList.questionTitle: Monopsony hiring, fewer workers, lower wage
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: In a monopsonistic labor market, a firm will continue to hire workers until the marginal revenue product of labor

optionList.optionContent: equals marginal factor (resource) cost, resulting in more workers being hired at a lower wage compared with a perfectly competitive labor market
equals marginal factor (resource) cost, resulting in fewer workers being hired at a lower wage compared with a perfectly competitive labor market
equals marginal factor (resource) cost, resulting in fewer workers being hired at a higher wage compared with a perfectly competitive labor market
is less than marginal factor (resource) cost, resulting in more workers being hired at a lower wage compared with a perfectly competitive labor market
is less than marginal factor (resource) cost, resulting in fewer workers being hired at a lower wage compared with a perfectly competitive labor market

questionOption:
A .

equals marginal factor (resource) cost, resulting in more workers being hired at a lower wage compared with a perfectly competitive labor market

B .

equals marginal factor (resource) cost, resulting in fewer workers being hired at a lower wage compared with a perfectly competitive labor market

C .

equals marginal factor (resource) cost, resulting in fewer workers being hired at a higher wage compared with a perfectly competitive labor market

D .

is less than marginal factor (resource) cost, resulting in more workers being hired at a lower wage compared with a perfectly competitive labor market

E .

is less than marginal factor (resource) cost, resulting in fewer workers being hired at a lower wage compared with a perfectly competitive labor market

questionList.questionTitle: Beyond profit maximizing output, MC exceeds MR
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Assume that a monopolistically competitive firm is currently maximizing profit with an output of 100 units and a price of $50. Which of the following is true?

optionList.optionContent: Total revenue is maximized when the firm produces 100 units of output.
Marginal revenue equals $50 when the firm produces 100 units of output.
Marginal cost is greater than marginal revenue when the firm produces 150 units of output.
Marginal cost is minimized when the firm produces 100 units of output.
Average total cost is minimized when the firm produces 100 units of output.

questionOption:
A .

Total revenue is maximized when the firm produces 100 units of output.

B .

Marginal revenue equals $50 when the firm produces 100 units of output.

C .

Marginal cost is greater than marginal revenue when the firm produces 150 units of output.

D .

Marginal cost is minimized when the firm produces 100 units of output.

E .

Average total cost is minimized when the firm produces 100 units of output.

questionList.questionTitle: Collusion tough because cheating firm benefits
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: There are four firms in an oligopolistic industry. The four firms agree to collude and act like a monopoly. If one of the firms violates the agreement and charges a lower price or sells a larger quantity than what was agreed to, what will happen in the short run?

optionList.optionContent: The firm that cheats will earn higher profits, and industry profits will be lower.
The firm that cheats will earn higher profits, and industry profits will be higher.
The firm that cheats will earn lower profits, and industry profits will be lower.
The firm that cheats will earn lower profits, and industry profits will be higher.
The firms that do not cheat will earn higher profits.

questionOption:
A .

The firm that cheats will earn higher profits, and industry profits will be lower.

B .

The firm that cheats will earn higher profits, and industry profits will be higher.

C .

The firm that cheats will earn lower profits, and industry profits will be lower.

D .

The firm that cheats will earn lower profits, and industry profits will be higher.

E .

The firms that do not cheat will earn higher profits.

questionList.questionTitle: Economies of scale create market power
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Which of the following can give a firm market power?

optionList.optionContent: Having access to common information
Producing a standardized or homogeneous product
Lacking barriers to entry or exit
Having a large number of competitors in the market
Having economies of scale in production over the range of market output

questionOption:
A .

Having access to common information

B .

Producing a standardized or homogeneous product

C .

Lacking barriers to entry or exit

D .

Having a large number of competitors in the market

E .

Having economies of scale in production over the range of market output

questionList.questionTitle: Advertising tries to create inelastic demand

questionList.choiceQuestionContent: The Lorenz curves in the graph above show the effects of a tax system on income inequality. Which of the following statements must be true?

optionList.optionContent: Before the tax, 20% of families lived below the poverty level, and after the tax, none did.
The tax redistributed income only from the top 20% of families to the lowest 20%.
The tax system is proportional.
The tax system is regressive.
The tax system is progressive.

questionOption:

Before the tax, 20% of families lived below the poverty level, and after the tax, none did.

B .

The tax redistributed income only from the top 20% of families to the lowest 20%.

C .

The tax system is proportional.

D .

The tax system is regressive.

E .

The tax system is progressive.

The relationship between the price of an input and the quantity demanded
The decrease in efficiency

A decrease in consumers’ income, assuming that beef is a normal good
An increase in the supply of potatoes, a complementary good
An increase in the price of corn, an input in the production of beef
An announcement by the medical community that consumption of beef increases the risk of heart disease

questionList.questionTitle: Mickey ups utility with more games, less songs
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Mickey has a $20 gift card that she is planning to use to download 10 songs and 5 games. The download price for each song is $1 and the download price for each game is $2. Her marginal utility from the tenth song is 30, and her marginal utility from the fifth game is 70. Based on this information, which of the following statements is true?

optionList.optionContent: Mickey’s plan will maximize her utility.
Mickey can increase utility by buying more songs and fewer games.
Mickey can increase utility by buying more games and fewer songs.
Since games are twice as expensive as songs, Mickey should buy twice as many songs.
Since games are twice as expensive as songs, Mickey should buy twice as many games.

questionOption:
A .

Mickey’s plan will maximize her utility.

B .

Mickey can increase utility by buying more songs and fewer games.

C .

Mickey can increase utility by buying more games and fewer songs.

D .

Since games are twice as expensive as songs, Mickey should buy twice as many songs.

E .

Since games are twice as expensive as songs, Mickey should buy twice as many games.

questionList.questionTitle: Mono comp long run equilibrium described
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Which of the following is true of a monopolistically competitive firm in long-run equilibrium?

optionList.optionContent: It produces where price equals marginal cost, and it earns zero economic profits.
It produces where marginal revenue exceeds marginal cost, and it earns positive economic profits.
It produces where marginal cost equals marginal revenue, and the price is equal to average total cost.
It produces at the minimum average total cost, and it utilizes all excess capacity.
It produces more than the allocatively efficient quantity, and the price is greater than marginal cost.

questionOption:
A .

It produces where price equals marginal cost, and it earns zero economic profits.

B .

It produces where marginal revenue exceeds marginal cost, and it earns positive economic profits.

C .

It produces where marginal cost equals marginal revenue, and the price is equal to average total cost.

D .

It produces at the minimum average total cost, and it utilizes all excess capacity.

E .

It produces more than the allocatively efficient quantity, and the price is greater than marginal cost.

questionList.questionTitle: Barbers' MRP decreases with drop in haircut prices
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Assume that barber shops operate in perfectly competitive product and factor markets. Which of the following will happen to working barbers if the price of haircuts decreases?

optionList.optionContent: The marginal product of the last barber hired will not change.
The marginal product of the last barber hired will decrease.
The marginal revenue product curve will shift to the right.
The marginal revenue product curve will shift to the left.
There will be a movement upward along the marginal revenue curve.

questionOption:
A .

The marginal product of the last barber hired will not change.

B .

The marginal product of the last barber hired will decrease.

C .

The marginal revenue product curve will shift to the right.

D .

The marginal revenue product curve will shift to the left.

E .

There will be a movement upward along the marginal revenue curve.

questionList.questionTitle: Definition of dominant strategy in duopoly
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: In a market with two firms, a firm that has a dominant strategy will do which of the following?

optionList.optionContent: Maintain that strategy independent of the strategies chosen by its competitor.
Adjust its strategies based on the strategies chosen by its competitor.
Make the first move, and wait to see whether its competitor responds to the move.
Keep its competitor guessing about its next move.
Collude with its competitor to reduce uncertainty.

questionOption:
A .

Maintain that strategy independent of the strategies chosen by its competitor.

B .

Adjust its strategies based on the strategies chosen by its competitor.

C .

Make the first move, and wait to see whether its competitor responds to the move.

D .

Keep its competitor guessing about its next move.

E .

Collude with its competitor to reduce uncertainty.

equal to its marginal cost and grant a subsidy to cover the loss

equal to its average total cost and levy a tax on the excess profit

C .

greater than its marginal cost and levy a tax on the excess profit

D .

greater than its marginal cost but that minimizes the deadweight loss

E .

greater than its average total cost but that minimizes the deadweight loss

questionList.questionTitle: Pencils made in perf. comp. mkt. in LR equilibrium

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Assume that pencils are manufactured in a perfectly competitive market that is in long-run equilibrium.

  1. Draw correctly labeled side-by-side graphs for the pencil market and for a representative firm and show each of the following.

    1. The market price and quantity, labeled PM and QM, respectively

    2. The firm’s profit-maximizing price and quantity, labeled PF and QF, respectively

  2. What is the relationship between PM and PF? Explain.

  3. Rent on the factory building is an important fixed cost in the production of pencils, and the industry experiences significant increases in rent.

    1. What will happen to the firm’s profit-maximizing quantity in the short run? Explain.

    2. On your graph in part (a), show the impact of the rent increase and completely shade the area representing the firm’s profit or loss in the short run.

  4. As a result of the rent increase, what will happen to each of the following in the long run?

    1. The number of firms in the market. Explain.

    2. The market equilibrium quantity relative to QM and the market equilibrium price relative to PM. Explain.

Directions: You are advised to spend the first 10 minutes reading all of the questions and planning your answers. You will then have 50 minutes to answer all three of the following questions. You may begin writing your responses before the reading period is over. It is suggested that you spend approximately half your time on the first question and divide the remaining time equally between the next two questions.Include correctly labeled diagrams, if useful or required, in explaining your answers. A correctly labeled diagram must have all axes and curves clearly labeled and must show directional changes. Use a pen with black or dark blue ink.
Respond to all parts of the question.

questionList.questionTitle: Graph that Zhao, Inc. faces in the zizzles market

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
1. The graph below depicts the market conditions Zhao, Inc., faces in the zizzles market, where D = demand, MSB = marginal social benefit, MR = marginal revenue, MSC = marginal social cost, and MPC = marginal private cost.

  1. What two potential sources of market failure exist in the market for Zhao’s zizzles? Explain each potential source of market failure using the information in the graph.

     

  2. Using numerical values from the graph, identify Zhao’s profit-maximizing quantity and price.

     

  3. Assume a $15 per-unit tax is imposed on the zizzles market. Using numerical values from the graph, identify Zhao’s profit-maximizing quantity and price.

     

  4. Zhao opposes the $15 per-unit tax analyzed above in part (c) and instead proposes a policy of no government intervention. If economic advisers are only interested in maximizing social welfare, would they support the policy of no government intervention? Explain.

Directions: You are advised to spend the first 10 minutes reading all of the questions and planning your answers. You will then have 50 minutes to answer all three of the following questions. You may begin writing your responses before the reading period is over. It is suggested that you spend approximately half your time on the first question and divide the remaining time equally between the next two questions.Include correctly labeled diagrams, if useful or required, in explaining your answers. A correctly labeled diagram must have all axes and curves clearly labeled and must show directional changes. Use a pen with black or dark blue ink.
Respond to all parts of the question.

questionList.questionTitle: Milk in perf. comp. mkt. with price floor

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Directions: You are advised to spend the first 10 minutes reading all of the questions and planning your answers. You will then have 50 minutes to answer all three of the following questions. You may begin writing your responses before the reading period is over. It is suggested that you spend approximately half your time on the first question and divide the remaining time equally between the next two questions.Include correctly labeled diagrams, if useful or required, in explaining your answers. A correctly labeled diagram must have all axes and curves clearly labeled and must show directional changes. Use a pen with black or dark blue ink.

Assume milk is produced and sold in a perfectly competitive market. To help milk producers, the government imposes an effective price floor on milk. Assume milk producers continue to sell milk only to households after the imposition of the price floor.

  1. Draw a correctly labeled graph for the milk market and show the price floor, labeled PF , and the quantity of milk purchased by households, labeled QF.

  2. Is the market for milk allocatively efficient after the price floor is imposed? Explain.

  3. On your graph from part (a), shade the area that represents total producer surplus after the price floor is imposed.

  4. Assume the absolute value of price elasticity of demand for milk is 2 throughout the price range. Would total consumer spending on milk increase, decrease, not change, or be indeterminate after the imposition of the price floor? Explain.

  5. Assume the price floor in the milk market caused the equilibrium price of eggs, a related consumption good, to increase.

    1. Draw a correctly labeled graph of the egg market, and show the effect of the price floor in the milk market on the egg market.

    2. What economic term describes the relationship between milk and egg?

Respond to all parts of the question.

partList.directionsText:
MICROECONOMICS
SECTION Ⅰ
Time - 1 hour and 10 minutes
60 Questions

 

Directions: Each of the questions or incomplete statements below is followed by five suggested answers or completions. Select the one that is best in each case and then fill in the corresponding circle on the answer sheet.

MICROECONOMICS
SECTION Ⅱ
Total Time - 1 hour
Reading Period - 10 minutes
Writing Period - 50 minutes
3 Questions


Directions: You are advised to spend the first 10 minutes reading all of the questions and planning your answers. You will then have 50 minutes to answer all three of the following questions. You may begin writing your responses before the reading period is over. lt is suggested that you spend approximately half your time on the first question and divide the remaining time equally between the next two questions. Include correctly labeled diagrams, if useful or required, in explaining your answers. A correctly labeled diagram must have all axes and curves clearly labeled and must show directional changes. lf the question prompts you to "Calculate," you must show how you arrived at your final answer. Use a pen with black or dark blue ink.

You may plan your answers in this orange booklet, but no credit will be given for anything written in this booklet. You will only earn credit for what you write in the separate Free Response booklet.
